coredata.dc:title: Electrocatalytic excitation and Co-reaction acceleration synergistic amplification signal of hydrazide-conjugated carbon dots for an electrochemiluminescence immunoassay
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Jinglong
Feng, Ruiqing
Yang, Lei
Yue, Qi
Li, Min
Zhang, Nuo
Dan Wu,
Ma, Hongmin
Cao, Wei
Wei, Qin
coredata.dc:description:
Biocompatible nitrogen-doped hydrazide-conjugated carbon dots (NHCDs) as an anodic luminophores with low excitation potential were equipped with an efficient multiple signal-amplification strategy for electrochemiluminescence (ECL) trace bioanalysis. To promote the ECL emission of NHCDs-H2O2 system, gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) functionalized tin oxide nanocrystals (Au@SnO2 NCs) were adopted to anchor large amount of NHCDs, the formed NHCDs-Au@SnO2 NCs could conjugate with detection antibody as the signal label. Moreover, 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic acid (PTCA) molecules were immobilized on the flower-like microsphere (PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO) nanocomposites to fabricate as an electro-active sensing substrate to incubate the capture antibody, which could efficiently accelerate the generation of reactive oxygen species (OH · /O2
·- ) through electrocatalytic excitation and co-reaction acceleration. Integrating with the sandwich-type sensing model, an ECL immunosensor was established to detect biomarkers of squamous cell carcinomas named cytokeratin 19 fragments (cyfra21–1). Combining with the synergistic signal-amplification induced by the Au@SnO2 NCs, PTCA, and ZnCo2O4/ZnO nanocomposites, the developed immunosensor performed detection range from 0.09 pg/mL to 100 ng/mL with a detection limit of 34.38 fg/mL. The results showcased the great potential of this sensing strategy for real sample analysis of biomarker with reliable stability, selectivity and repeatability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Synergistic signal-amplification
Anode low potential luminescent carbon dots
Electrocatalytic excitation
Co-reaction acceleration

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ECL Responses of cyfra21-1 via biosensor
Stability, specificity and repeatability of sensors

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Jinan Scientific Research Leader Workshop Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020YQ13 , ZR2019BB007 , ZR2020QB097 ), Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Youth Innovation Science and Technology Program ( 2020KJC008 ), the Jinan Scientific Research Leader Workshop Project ( 2020GXRC048 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD of ZnCo2O4/ZnO, (B) SEM of ZnCo2O4/ZnO, (C) SEM of PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO, (D) XRD of SnO2 NCs, (E) SEM of SnO2 NCs, (F) SEM of Au@SnO2 NCs.
Mechanistic illustrations of NHCDs-H2O2 without any co-reaction accelerator (A), with Au@SnO2 NCs as co-reaction accelerator (B), and with PTCA@ZnCo2O4 as co-reaction accelerator (C).

CV curve of NHCDs-ZnCo2O4/ZnO and NHCDs-PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO without H2O2 (A) and with H2O2 (B), ECL-time curve of NHCDs-ZnCo2O4/ZnO and NHCDs-PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO with H2O2 (C), CV curve of NHCDs-SnO2 and NHCDs-Au@SnO2 NCs without H2O2 (D) and with H2O2 (E), ECL-time curve of NHCDs-SnO2 and NHCDs-Au@SnO2 NCs with H2O2 (F).

EIS plots (A) and CV (B) of the electrode under different modified states: GCE (a), Ab1-PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO/GCE (b), BSA/Ab1-PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO/GCE (c), cyfra21–1/BSA/Ab1-PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO/GCE (d), Ab2-NHCDs-Au@SnO2 NCs/cyfra21–1/BSA/Ab1-PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO/GCE (e).

ECL response (A) and Calibration plots (B) of the immunosensor for the detection of different concentrations of cyfra21–1: (a) 0.1 pg/mL, (b) 1 pg/mL, (c) 10 pg/mL, (d) 0.1 ng/mL, (e) 1 ng/mL, (f) 10 ng/mL, (g) 50 ng/mL, (h) 75 ng/mL, (i) 100 ng/ mL, error bar =RSD (n = 5).
The schematic illustration of the electrochemiluminescence immunosensor.

head.ce:title: Electrocatalytic excitation and Co-reaction acceleration synergistic amplification signal of hydrazide-conjugated carbon dots for an electrochemiluminescence immunoassay

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Biocompatible nitrogen-doped hydrazide-conjugated carbon dots (NHCDs) as an anodic luminophores with low excitation potential were equipped with an efficient multiple signal-amplification strategy for electrochemiluminescence (ECL) trace bioanalysis. To promote the ECL emission of NHCDs-H2O2 system, gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) functionalized tin oxide nanocrystals (Au@SnO2 NCs) were adopted to anchor large amount of NHCDs, the formed NHCDs-Au@SnO2 NCs could conjugate with detection antibody as the signal label. Moreover, 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic acid (PTCA) molecules were immobilized on the flower-like microsphere (PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO) nanocomposites to fabricate as an electro-active sensing substrate to incubate the capture antibody, which could efficiently accelerate the generation of reactive oxygen species (OH · /O2
·- ) through electrocatalytic excitation and co-reaction acceleration. Integrating with the sandwich-type sensing model, an ECL immunosensor was established to detect biomarkers of squamous cell carcinomas named cytokeratin 19 fragments (cyfra21–1). Combining with the synergistic signal-amplification induced by the Au@SnO2 NCs, PTCA, and ZnCo2O4/ZnO nanocomposites, the developed immunosensor performed detection range from 0.09 pg/mL to 100 ng/mL with a detection limit of 34.38 fg/mL. The results showcased the great potential of this sensing strategy for real sample analysis of biomarker with reliable stability, selectivity and repeatability.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Anode low potential luminescent carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a burgeoning and effective analytical method for ultrasensitive detection of disease-related biomarkers because of its simple operation, high sensitivity, and great controllability [1–4]. In order to improve the sensitivity of ECL, the exploration of low potential luminophores has gradually become a hot issue [5–7]. Luminophores excited by low potential can attenuate the redox of proteins or nucleotides in biomarkers as much as possible during the detection process, and the results are guaranteed to be more accurate and stable. For instance, a biosensor equipped with bovine serum albumin (BSA)-stabilized gold nanoclusters (BSA@Au NCs) as low potential luminophores has been studied and achieved the early sensitive detection of procalcitonin [8]. In the meantime, a biocompatible electrochemiluminescence platform, employing nitrogen-doped hydrazide-conjugated carbon dots (NHCDs) prepared through a one-step solvothermal protocol as low potential ECL emitter [9,10], was proposed for the rapid detection of living cancer cells. With the use of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) from cancer cells as the co-reactant, luminol-like units on the surface of NHCDs can generate extremely strong anode ECL emission. Compared with noble metal clusters, obviously, integrating the advantages of high stability, high emission efficiency and low cost, NHCDs have broader application prospects. Thus, it is worth looking forward to develop a new ECL strategy or system based on NHCDs for analysis and detection.

Currently, there are many signal change strategies acting on ECL analysis, such as surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [11,12], resonance energy transfer (RET) [13–15], co-reaction accelerator (CRA) [16,17] and so on. With superiority of easy fabrication, high sensitivity and easy to understand, CRA system has become the focus of researchers [8,16,18]. And transition metal oxides have attracted great interests in CRA-ECL biosensing owing to their simple synthesis, stable chemical properties, designable structure and co-reaction catalytic performance [17,19]. It has been found that zinc oxide (ZnO) has excellent ability to amplify ECL response as the co-reaction accelerator, indicating that the nanomaterial is a good candidate for establishing an ECL system [20,21]. Nevertheless, the extremely low water dispersion properties make the support of nano carrier materials extremely needed. Among the various sensing substrates, zinc cobaltite (ZnCo2O4) has become a new favorite because of its high theoretical capacity and environmental friendliness [22,23]. It is worth emphasizing that the ZnCo2O4 can effectively catalyze the redox of the H2O2 [24]. Therefore, it is feasible to combine the two materials and apply the composite materials to the construction of sensors. On the strength of this, nanosheet stacked flower-like microspheres ZnCo2O4/ZnO were successfully synthesized through a simple and rapid hydrothermal protocol [25]. Considering the improvement of compatibility, 3,4,9,10-Perylenetetracarboxylic Acid (PTCA) [26] was employed as linking agent for subsequent assembly with capture antibodies. Surprisingly, we discovered that PTCA can be used in the co-reaction acceleration strategies as electrocatalytic excitation accelerator due to the π-network plane in PTCA molecule [27–29]. Electrocatalytic excitation proposed by Liu can accelerate the excitation process of luminophores on the surface of solid-state sensor and further improve the ECL efficiency [29]. The catalytic activity of the composite sensing platform (PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO), depending on the electrocatalytic excitation and co-reaction synergistic amplification from PTCA molecules and flower-like ZnCo2O4/ZnO microstructures, was significantly developed compared with that of pure ZnCo2O4/ZnO.

In order to construct stable combination and prepare signal multiple-amplification system, and then realize highly sensitive detection, the exploration and design of novel nanocarrier are urgently needed. Tin oxide (SnO2), a metal oxide semiconductor material, has been extensively studied as an electrode support material due to its advantages of high electron mobility and low cost [30–32]. More importantly, numerous documents show that SnO2 has a strong catalytic effect on the decomposition of H2O2 while there was no report of its co-reaction accelerator effect in ECL system [33–36]. On this basis, tin oxide nanocrystals (SnO2 NCs) with (221) facets exposed were synthesized by hydrothermal method [37]. Furthermore, gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) were used to enhance conductivity and utilized as a linking agent for capture antibodies. The prepared luminescent composites achieve 2.3-fold stronger ECL output than pure NHCDs.

Lung cancer is a disease with high incidence rate and mortality rate in the world [38]. Early diagnosis is still the best way to find, treat and prolong the survival time of lung cancer patients. Cytokeratin 19 fragment 21–1 (cyfra21–1), a soluble fragment of cytokeratin 19, is released into the blood during apoptosis. and is an important biomarker of non-small cell carcinoma and a significant reference index for early diagnosis of lung cancer [39–42]. Herein, a signal multiple-amplification ECL biosensor integrating high catalytic performance sensing substrate and highly stable biocompatibility signal indicator was established to detect cyfra21–1. SnO2 NCs was surface-modified by Au NPs to carry low potential NHCDs and conjugate detecting antibodies. Considering the stability and enzyme mimic property, PTCA, which was utilized as a biocompatible catalytic substrate for capturing antibodies, was evenly compounded on the surface of flower-like ZnCo2O4/ZnO microstructures. Under the synergistic amplification of electrocatalytic excitation and co-reaction acceleration, the as-proposed ECL immunosensor achieved ultra-sensitive determination for cyfra21–1 with a low limit of detection down to 34.38 fg/mL. Compared with previous work [2,43–45], the system can detect markers at low excitation potential and avoid the redox interference of biomarkers or matrix. Electrocatalytic excitation further accelerates the excitation process of luminophores and reduces the content of H2O2 as much as possible to achieve sensitive detection. The strategy adopted is expected to play a role in the determination of trace proteins in the early stage of cancer.
The reagents and instruments are described in the Supporting Information.

For a typical hydrothermal synthesis [25], 0.184 g Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O), 0.498 g cobalt(II) acetate tetrahydrate (Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O), 0.39 g urea, and 0.1 g ammonium fluoride (NH4F) were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized (DI) water in an orderly manner. Then stirred for 30 min to form a pink transparent solution. After that, the pink transparent solution was poured into a teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with a capacity of 50 mL, and it was hydrothermally reacted at 120 °C for 1 h. It was naturally cooled after the reaction was completed. Collected the precipitate by centrifugation, washed it several times with deionized water and absolute ethanol, respectively. Hereafter dried overnight at 60 °C in a vacuum drying oven. Finally, the obtained precursor was annealed in the air at a heating rate of 2 °C/min at 450 °C for 4 h to obtain a porous ZnCo2O4/ZnO flower-like microstructure.

3,4,9,10-Perylenetetracarboxylic (PTCA) was ultrasonically dispersed in ultrapure water to obtain a 1 mg/mL solution, and then ZnCo2O4/ZnO (30 mg) was dispersed in an aqueous solution of PTCA (10 mL) and the ultrasonic was continued for 10 min. Then, the mixture was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 120 °C for 12 h. Finally, collected the precipitate by centrifugation, washed it several times with deionized water and absolute ethanol, respectively. Hereafter dried overnight at 55 °C in a vacuum drying oven. The target products (PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO) were obtained. After that, 5 mg PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO, 500 μL of freshly prepared PBS mixed with crosslinker (pH 7.4, EDC 30 mmol and NHS 15 mmol) and 500 μL Ab1 (10 μg/mL) were mixed and incubated at 4 °C with shaking for 24 h. Afterwards, the biological complex obtained by high-speed centrifugation was then washed once with PBS, and finally, the washed substrate was dissolved in 1 mL of PBS for later use.

Tin oxide nanocrystallites (SnO2 NCs) were synthesized by the classical hydrothermal method [37] and the details were described in Supporting Information. Au@SnO2 NCs were prepared by the in-situ redox. Typically, 30 mg as-prepared SnO2 NCs powder were uniformly dispersed in 10.0 mL ultrapure water by ultrasonic treatment, then 4.33 mL chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) aqueous solution (2%) were added to the suspension and magnetic stirring was continued until it was evenly dispersed. Next, 1.00 mL ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O) was added dropwise to the mixture. After stirring for 3 h, the sediment was collected by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 5 min and washed for three times with deionized water and dried at 55 °C for 12 h.

The nitrogen doped hydrazide-conjugated carbon dots (NHCDs) were synthesized by a reported hydrothermal synthesis method [9,10] with minor modifications, which was described in the Supporting Information. NHCDs was added into Au@SnO2 NCs (1 mg/mL,5 mL) solution and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. Then, the precipitate was obtained by centrifuging at 9000 rpm for 5 min and washed three times and dispersed in 1 mL PBS. After that, 500 μL of 10 μg/mL Ab2 solution was added under stirring at 4 °C for 12 h. The obtained solution was centrifuged and washed once with PBS. Finally, the substance after washing was dispersed in 1 mL of PBS and stored at 4 °C for further use.

GCE was polished using 0.3 and 0.05 µm Al2O3 powder, then washed with ultrapure water and dried under a nitrogen atmosphere. Then 6 μL of Ab1-PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO bioconjugate was dripped onto the prepared GCE surface and then the modified electrode was placed at 37 °C until the PBS on the surface nearly disappeared. After that, 3 μL of 1% BSA solution was dropped on the electrode at 37 °C for 1 h to block the nonspecific binding sites. After drying, the electrode was washed with deionized water. Next, 8 μL of cyfra21–1 at different concentrations was dropped on the modified electrode with specific recognition ability, and the electrode was incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. Finally, when the liquid on the electrode surface was about to disappear, 8 μL Ab2-NHCDs-Au@SnO2 NCs bioconjugate was covered on the electrode, and then it underwent specific recognition immune response at 37 °C. After the electrode was dried, the unbound markers were washed out with deionized water. The biosensor had been fully constructed and stored at 4 °C for subsequent testing. Scheme 1 introduced the manufacturing process of the proposed biosensor.

In 10 mL of mixed electrolyte (1/15 M PBS, pH 7.8, 30 mM H2O2), the immunosensor was tested by a three-electrode system. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used for the electrochemical measurement from 0 to 0.80 V (vs Ag/AgCl) to generate ECL signal. After filtering the optimal conditions, the ECL responses to cyfra21–1 with different concentrations were recorded.

Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) was employed to observe the morphology of ZnCo2O4/ZnO. The overall morphology of ZnCo2O4/ZnO synthesized was a flower-like microsphere with a diameter of about 7–8 µm ( Fig. 1B, Fig. S1), and its surface was an embedded structure of nanosheets, indicating that the material had a very high specific surface area. Correspondingly, it was proved by mapping (Fig. S2) that the metal elements contained in the sample were Co, Zn. The crystal structure of synthesized sample was identified by comparing XRD patterns with standard cards. As shown in Fig. 1A, the prepared sample contained exactly ZnCo2O4 and ZnO. The peaks marked with blue symbols corresponded to the standard diffraction peaks of wurtzite hexagonal ZnO (JCPDS NO.36–1451) and the peaks marked with red symbols corresponded to the standard diffraction peaks of cubic ZnCo2O4 (JCPDS NO.23–1390). All results proved the successful preparation of ZnCo2O4/ZnO composite materials. Pure PTCA certified by XRD (Fig. S3). Furthermore, the synthesis of PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO was confirmed by SEM (Fig. 1C), mapping (Fig. S4) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. 1C, the surface nanosheets of ZnCo2O4/ZnO were covered by nanoparticles. And mapping (Fig. S4) proved that the particles were multi-carbon material. In order to prove that the material composite material can capture Ab1, infrared spectroscopy was tested to confirm the functional groups on the surface of the material. It could be seen from the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S5) that there was carboxyl on the material for antibody capture.

Fig. 1D and Fig. 1E respectively exhibited the XRD pattern and SEM image of SnO2 NCs. According to the results of the picture, the diffraction peaks of the synthesized material corresponded to the characteristic peaks of the standard tetragonal rutile SnO2 card (JCPDS No.41–1445), proving that the synthesis of SnO2 NCs was successful. The appearance of SnO2 NCs was a smooth dodecahedron with a diameter of about 150 nm and a width of about 100 nm. There were four triangular planes at each end and four rectangular miniature planes in the middle. The synthesis of Au@SnO2 NCs was characterized by SEM and EDS elemental analysis. Gold nanoparticles with a particle size of about 5 nm were uniformly distributed on the surface of SnO2 NCs (Fig. 1F). The mapping image (Fig. S6) illustrated the existence of the Au element. In addition, EDS analysis (Fig. S7) showed that the Au content in the material satisfied the load of NHCDs and the capture of antibodies. As shown in Fig. S9, the UV-Vis absorption spectra showed that NHCDs were successfully loaded on the nanocarriers. TEM was hired to observe the morphology of NHCDs. As shown in Fig. S11, monodisperse particles with an average diameter of about 3 nm could be observed, indicating that the prepared NHCDs had good dispersibility and homogeneity.

The luminescence properties and potential selection of NHCDs were put into the support information. The NHCDs - H2O2 system ECL emission mechanism exploration were based on reported literature [9] and showed equation below (Eqs. 1–4, 9, 10). The excited NHCDs were generated on the electrode surface through redox pathway, light was emitted when the excited NHCDs returned to the ground state. And when the ECL process was changed by Au@SnO2 NCs and PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO, the production of ROSs was significantly improved, and excellent signal amplification was achieved. On this basis, the possible mechanism of the sensor was speculated as follows: i) In Route 2, H2O2 was adsorbed onto the surface catalytic sites of Au@SnO2 NCs, and Sn4+ reacted with H2O2 to form per hydroxyl radical (HO2
· ) [35,36,46], which could further react with H2O2 to form OH · along with the extra generation of O2 (Eq. 5). Finally, the generated species could participate in and accelerate the formation of excited NHCDs. ii) Due to the splitting of energy band in ZnCo2O4/ZnO, the electron hole (e-/h+) couple was generated [47]. which could transfer electrons with the adsorbed species on the surface of the sensing platform. Here, the electron hole (e-/h+) couple guided the faster redox process of the co-reactant to produce high oxidation free radicals (Eq. 6), and then enlarged the ECL emission. At the same time, the OH · generated in this process undergone electron transfer with the double bonds existing in the PTCA plane [28,48], and then produced PTCA π-conjugated carbon radicals (π-CC(PTCA) · ), which could react with NHCDs to accelerate the formation of NHCDs anionic radicals (Eqs. 7, 8) and finally more excited NHCDs were obtained. Fig. 2.

The proposed ECL immunosensor was based on the synergistic signal amplification induced by the electrocatalytic excitation and co-reaction acceleration. Therefore, the exploration of the role of accelerators was of vital importance. To demonstrate the enzyme-mimic activity of PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO, the CV curve ( Figs. 3A and 3B) and the synchronized ECL response (Fig. 3C) were measured in PBS. The current response of pure CDs in the presence of 30 mmol/L H2O2 (Fig. 3A) had a significant peak increase and appearance change compared to the test performance in PBS without H2O2 (Fig. 3A), indicating that H2O2 participated in the luminescence reaction as a co-reactant. Taking the test result as the standard, the performance of the material was measured and compared. When ZnCo2O4/ZnO/NHCDs/GCE (Fig. 3B) were tested with H2O2, the anode current increased and the oxidation curve appeared earlier. Which could be considered as the result of co-reaction promotion, accelerating the generation of OH · /O2
·- that can react with NHCDs · . Then, PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO/NHCDs/GCE (Fig. 3B) was tested with H2O2, and the current peak was further enhanced by the participation of PTCA, indicating that the electrocatalytic excitation of the substrate greatly improved the catalytic activity. Furthermore, ECL intensity curves of NHCDS/GCE, ZnCo2O4/ZnO/NHCDs/GCE and PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO/NHCDs/GCE tested in PBS with H2O2 were shown in Fig. 3C. The modification of the proposed co-reaction accelerator ZnCo2O4/ZnO made the signal intensity stronger, and the role of PTCA was also reflected. Which was consistent with the expression of the above CV curve. All the above results proved that PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO played a vital role as an efficient accelerator to promote the ECL emission of NHCDs.

Simultaneously, the coordinated CV curve (Figs. 3D and 3E) and ECL response (Fig. 3F) of Au@SnO2 NCs/NHCDs were also measured in PBS. By comparing the CV curves of NHCDs/GCE, SnO2 NCs/NHCDs/GCE and Au@SnO2 NCs/NHCDs/GCE, both SnO2 NCs and Au NPs could make the oxidation current appear early and made it reach a higher oxidation peak. Reflected in the ECL intensity curve, the acceleration of SnO2 NCs derived from redox and the conductivity enhancement of Au NPs both increased the peak value of the signal. The above results all proved that Au@SnO2 NCs as a co-reaction accelerator had a good catalytic decomposition effect of H2O2 and promotes the redox reaction of NHCDs ·- . Based on the guidance of the electrocatalytic excitation and co-reaction amplification from the substrate and the label, the multiple-enhancement strategy was adopted to construct an ECL immunosensor for the sensitive detection of cyfra21–1.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) and CV were adopted to confirm the stepwise characterization of the assembled immunosensor. The impedance spectrum of GCE under different modification steps were detected in 10 mL [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- (2.5 mmol/L) solution containing KCl (0.1 mol/L), and the results were shown in Fig. 4A. Compared with the bare GCE (curve a), Ab1-PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO/GCE (curve b) showed a larger semicircle, which was due to the inherent conductivity of the oxide and the electron blocking effect of the non-conductive proteins. And after the subsequent assembly of non-conductive BSA and cyfra21–1, resistance (curves c and d) increased gradually. When the Ab2-NHCDs-Au@SnO2 NCs bioconjugate was modified (curve e), the resistance eventually increased to the maximum value. To further confirm, CV were measured in [Fe(CN)6]3- (5 mmol/L) solution containing KNO3 (0.1 mol/L). The results shown in the CV image and the results of EIS were trending synchronously. The gradual decrease of the peak current corresponds to the gradual increase of the impedance value, indicating that the gradual surface modification of the proposed ECL immunosensor was successful.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL Responses of cyfra21-1 via biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the optimal experimental conditions, ECL respond of different concentrations of cyfra21–1 were recorded by the proposed ECL sensing platform. The signal strength of the immunosensor gradually increased as the concentration of cyfra21–1 increased ( Fig. 5A) and was positively correlated with the logarithmic concentration of cyfra21–1 from 0.09 pg/mL to 100 ng/mL (Fig. 5B). The linear regression equation was I ECL = 1581.69 lg c + 8262.09 (unit of c is ng/mL) and the square correlation coefficient was 0.993. After calculation, the detection limit of 34.38 fg/mL (S / N = 3) was lower than other cyfra21–1 methods reported (Supporting information, Table S1), proving that the ECL immunosensor constructed by the multiple-enhancement strategy has advantages in the detection of cyfra21–1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, specificity and repeatability of sensors
ce:section.ce:para: In order to apply the proposed ECL immunosensor to actual sample analysis, the stability, specificity and repeatability were tested separately, and the graph was drawn for feasibility analysis. As shown in Fig. S15A, the biosensor had good stability that can meet the application under 13 CV scans. To investigate the specificity of the biosensor, five different biomarkers were used as interfering substances, including alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), β-amyloid protein (Aβ), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), insulin (Insulin) and Prostate-specific antigen (PSA). As shown in Fig. S15B, the proposed biosensor had a significant ECL response to the target cyfra21–1 but no obvious ECL response to interfering substances, indicating that the biosensor had the specificity for cyfra21–1 analysis. Furthermore, reproducibility has been proven through multiple batch-to-batch measurements. Fig. S15C showed that the signal values obtained from multiple inter-batch tests were highly consistent, and it could be concluded that the reproducibility of the sensor meets the requirements of actual analysis.

With the purpose of verifying the practicality of the constructed sensor, normal human serum samples were tested by the proposed immunosensor and the results were confirmed using the standard addition method. Normal human serum was first prepared and recorded, and then the test samples of different concentrations were prepared by adding different amounts of standard CYFRA 21–1 samples. All samples were detected by the developed biosensor and the recovery rate was analyzed. As shown in Table S2, the content of cyfra21–1 in the serum sample was 0.97 ng/mL, and the results of the sensor determination by standard addition method showed that the relative standard deviation was 3.9–4.5%, and the recovery rate was 90–110%, indicating that the multiple-enhancement strategy ECL sensing platform has potential application value in clinical analysis.

In summary, flower-like microsphere ZnCo2O4/ZnO was synthesized by hydrothermal method, and a multiple-enhancement biologically compatible ECL immunosensor was developed to detect cyfra21–1 based on the electrocatalytic excitation and co-reaction synergistic signal amplification of PTCA@ZnCo2O4/ZnO and SnO2 NCs. The immunosensor obtained good stability, specificity and repeatability. Electrocatalytic excitation guided by π-conjugated carbon radicals provides more alternatives for the selection of sensing platform and the establishment of detection scheme. Although the slightly complex construction process may affect the detection effect, it is believed that this sensing strategy can be used to detect other biomarkers and establish a potential tool for early diagnosis of certain diseases.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020YQ13, ZR2019BB007, ZR2020QB097), Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Youth Innovation Science and Technology Program (2020KJC008), the Jinan Scientific Research Leader Workshop Project (2020GXRC048).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131443.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jinglong Fang studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as a doctoral student.

Ruiqing Feng obtained his Ph.D. at 2017 in the State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials, Shandong University. His main research interest are the design and synthesis of biocompatible fluorescent dyes with large two-photon absorption cross sections, the impact of various medicines on organelles, and chemical sensors and biosensors.
Lei Yang studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as a doctoral student.
Qi Yue studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as a postgraduate student.
Min Li studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as a postgraduate student.

Nuo Zhang received her B.S. degree in biological technology from University of Jinan in 2007 and M.S. degree in analytical chemistry from University of Jinan in 2010. Now, she is a lecturer at University of Jinan. Her main research interests are electrochemical biosensors and photoelectrochemical biosensors

Dan Wu a professor and received the D.S. degree from Shandong University in 2005. She dedicates to the surfactant and biological macromolecules interaction. And now she also studies the role of surfactant in electrochemical immunosensor.

Hongmin Ma* received both his B.S. and M.S. degree in Applied Chemistry from University of Jinan in 2005 and 2008 respectively. And he has received his Ph.D. degree in Colloid and Interface Chemistry at Shandong University, investigating self-assembly at all scales at surfaces in 2011. Now, he is a professor at University of Jinan, interested in the assembly of nano-composites and the construction of ordered porous films as well as their analytical applications.
Wei Cao* is a Professor. He received his Ph.D. degree from Shandong University, has been engaged in analytic chemistry research. He has published over 60 research papers.

Qin Wei, a professor and D.Sc., has devoted herself to analytical teaching and scientific research. Her main research interests are the determination of protein and nucleic acid by photometry and the electrochemical immunosensor preparation. She has published over one hundred articles on analysis, immunosensor and applied successfully for many research projects.
